chapters.title: A good leader can function in turbulent times
chapters.text:
The United States has a proud past that has been pivotal in the history of civilization in modern times. The U.S. also boasts excellent policies and laws, making it an attractive and fruitful soil for anyone looking for opportunities. America is the jewel of the new world, and everyone wants a taste of it.

The U.S. achieved this status because of its extraordinary list of astounding presidents that have graced the Oval Office, each leaving an astonishing legacy. We cannot speak of all, so we'll focus on a few who represented or pioneered the general recurring themes and styles: Abraham Lincoln, Theodore Roosevelt, Franklin D. Roosevelt, and Lyndon B. Johnson. These great presidents left templates for their successors to follow and improve on.

The achievements of these leaders are even more remarkable, considering they had to take office in turbulent times. Leadership in such times tests not only the office but also the leader. Overcoming challenges is what determines if a leader is capable.

The actual quality of a great leader is how well they perform under pressure.

While other leaders may not have had the same challenges, these American presidents stepped up. They fought many wars, some with the pen and others on the battlefield, leading the country and the world, albeit indirectly, to a better future.

This summary will give a detailed account of these presidents, their challenges, and the unique traits that helped them overcome their hurdles. All came out on top because they cultivated superior tactics effective for the problems they had to solve.

chapters.title: Ways to be a leader
chapters.text:
There are many styles of leadership, but let's start with transformational leadership. This type refers to policies and laws that outrightly transform a nation, its economics, culture, and people. It is a sort of bloodless revolution that sometimes causes bloodshed.

Transformational leadership seeks to improve and transform the lives of the populace. Such a leader can affect the lives of his followers in several ways: mentally, physically, and socially. Take Abraham Lincoln, for example; he led the revolution that would eventually eradicate the plague of slavery and usher in a new society in America. This move meant that when the slavery-friendly South and the free North went to war, Lincoln gave the prize of freeing the enslaved people and ending slavery. A grand goal is always central to every transformational leader’s administration.

Abraham Lincoln’s ambition for change came from a young age. He was always perceptive and empathetic. He spent years educating himself, wishing for a better future, and waiting for his chance to act. When he moved to New Salem, he worked as a clerk in a general store and was always kind and candid with the customers, winning their hearts. He dreamed of a political career and planned to support poor communities. Most importantly, he’d recognize his ignorance, learn, and change for the better.

Lincoln knew his country needed drastic changes so that everyone had an equal chance to succeed. This core value guided his career.

Transformational leadership is effective because it inspires followers to identify with something larger than themselves, such as their country or its ideals.

Leaders set goals with values more significant than individuals.

By this definition, transformational leaders achieve their goals by making them about the country rather than themselves or their administration scorecard. They appeal to the citizens’ values and love of government to reach a national goal in the population’s best interest in the long run. Transformational leadership works perfectly on its own, but the case of Lincoln demonstrates that he added another style to enhance its potential — transactional leadership.

Did you know? George Washington was the only president unanimously elected, meaning every state representative voted for him.

chapters.title: A leadership of transactions
chapters.text:
Unlike transformational leadership, which favors the people, there is a closely related yet warped form — transactional leadership. This kind of leadership feeds off the citizens’ personal short or long-term interests. It acts as a transaction of citizen’s support for smooth running, hence the name.

Transactional leadership exists because people have needs, and somebody must meet them.

A transactional leader appeals to the pressing needs of the public and channels them into achieving a national goal. Such leaders are often gifted with great wit and guile and can speak with conviction, driving people to act, albeit selfishly. Countries run by such leaders enjoy the benefits of a leader who listens to the needs of their people.

Countries with transactional leaders are easy to spot. They reward their citizens for their service to the nation. However, this leadership style relies heavily on compromise, bargaining, and rewards for following through.

Transactional leadership follows democratic rule — the people’s government, for the people, and by the people.

Lincoln applied both styles — transformational and transactional — to advocate for emancipation. When he visited areas with extensive Copperhead influence, he appealed to the practical benefits of having black troops by emphasizing the transformational vantage point that black people contribute to the nation’s well-being and thus deserve freedom. He addressed the needs of the people while promoting a greater moral question and successfully encouraged soldiers to support emancipation.

The American soldiers were loyal to the nation and Lincoln’s leadership regarding his accessibility and accountability. They committed to the now unified purpose of the Union and Emancipation.

Eventually, thousands of Black People enlisted in the military, as Lincoln’s administration supported their civil rights. General James G. Blunt reported the black soldiers fought with astonishing vigor.

During the next election, Lincoln only wanted to win if he got most of the soldiers’ votes so the fight could continue, which he did. So successful was the use of the two leadership styles that soldiers chose the national goal instead of their self-interest. While legally securing the end of slavery was challenging, Lincoln ensured emancipation in the United States. His adaptability and devotion to democracy earned him overwhelming support and everlasting recognition.

chapters.title: How to lead during a crisis
chapters.text:
While transactional leadership brings the people into a synced state, it does not suffice in a chaotic state of society due to conflicting interests. A leader cannot rely on this style as it can overwhelm them when facing a crisis.

Crisis management is an essential part of governance; without it, no leadership style can succeed.

A leader with excellent crisis management skills is Theodore Roosevelt Jr. His decisiveness during the “Great Coal Strike” saw the nation through a severe economic and social crisis.

Roosevelt announced a new political era on his first day of presidency. A tireless and ambitious leader, he offered a fresh perspective on the problems of the United States. The Great Coal Strike was a working-class rebellion that set panic loose in the nation. Coal was the country’s primary power source then, and failing to resolve the strike would have been drastic. But the strike was unprecedented, and Roosevelt couldn’t legally interfere.

While he was encouraged to stay out of the free market, Roosevelt couldn’t remain passive, adopting Lincoln’s view of a president as a servant of the people. After compiling a thorough report on the circumstances of the strike, Roosevelt began acting methodically.

He suggested a controversial antitrust suit and reevaluated it after receiving feedback. Public opinion on the matter was critical — when the nation appealed to the government, the president had leeway to act in full force. He carefully chose a team and intervened, deploying the Pennsylvania State Guards, which sent some strikers back to work. The situation called for drastic measures; Roosevelt called for federal troops, and although the invasion never happened, his radical actions led to success.

Crisis management saves a nation from an array of problems.

Crisis management is a skill that sets the best leaders apart and helps with nation-building, but how does it work? If you wish to learn effective crisis management, follow these steps:

• Educate yourself on the causes and circumstances of the crisis.
• Create an emergency management team.
• Identify each step of your strategies and prepare a plan B.
• Acknowledge those who contributed to the solution.
• Document the process for future reference.

The habits of a vigorous mind are formed in contending with difficulties. Great necessities call out great virtues. ~ Abigail Adams

chapters.title: Creating hope where there is none
chapters.text:
A country will face insurmountable challenges for an extended period when its leader or that leader’s predecessor(s) lack crisis management skills. This lack can cause chaos in the nation, which makes governance more difficult as the government needs to maintain its connection with the masses.

The turnaround leadership style exists for this kind of situation. A turnaround leader takes on a seemingly hopeless problem and turns it into a flourishing one.

The Great Depression was a dark time in American history that arose from the famous stock market crash of 1929. This crash would drastically decrease productivity, causing a reduction in jobs by at least six million. But Franklin D. Roosevelt (FDR) turned it all around.

FDR did the unimaginable when he took office; he turned the tide and restored normalcy for the American economy. How? He took the bull by the horns and charged the legislature to draft laws to rejuvenate the country’s industrial and agricultural sectors. This rejuvenation led to increased jobs and a boost in the economy. Moreover, FDR didn’t just make laws; he kept the citizens informed and gave them hope through regular transmissions, later termed “fire-side chats.” These chats and affirmative legislative action saw a turnaround in the economy.

A turnaround leader can save a sinking economy and turn it into a flourishing utopia.

It is an excellent feat of leadership to right a sinking ship. A turnaround leader is reactionary and always takes the practical side, doing whatever works as the situation continues. Informing the public is also essential. The nation needs to understand its leader’s actions, their importance, and proposed plans in plain language.

To effectively pull a turnaround as a leader, you must:

• Make a clear distinction between previous circumstances and a new plan of action.
• Discuss expectations from both sides.
• Assemble people passionate about change.
• Engage the stakeholders.
• Stay flexible to adapt to changing circumstances.

Did you know? The average family income decreased by 40% during the Great Depression. At the same time, the unemployment rate was 3% compared to 3.8% in 2023.

chapters.title: Visionaries who build the future
chapters.text:
The ability to get a glimpse of the future is essential in leadership. With more and more countries facing crisis and financial instability, there is a need to implement a leadership style addressing this problem specifically.

Governance is complex in states overrun by prolonged crises or intense unrest.

Here is the place of visionary leadership. Like the transformational style, visionary leadership aims to better overall realities, but it won’t start immediately. The innovative leader plans and acts in anticipation of the future result; everything gets geared towards actualizing a small yet packed project.

A visionary leader acts and plans. Armed with the gift of foresight, an innovative leader sets the pace and places the right pieces of the puzzle for it to manifest in the future. They often get misunderstood if the public gets impatient.

A visionary leader needs to do the following for his vision to be adopted and successful:

• Gather support for a clear target.
• Enforce responsibility within the hierarchy.
• Inspire the team by visualizing positive results.
• Allow stakeholders to affect the actions from the beginning.

Such a leader needs a vision, a team that believes in and supports the idea, and a readiness to reward loyalty. Without a team, the dream dies a natural death. To ensure the leader’s vision is understood and adopted by all, they must show their followers the potential future and identify their role. Following Kennedy’s assassination, Lyndon Johnson faced chaos in the country. But he was eager to learn from Kennedy’s team and lead the country to a thriving state. He first delivered a speech to gain trust and prove his competency. Then, he focused on breaking the legislative limits that prevented Kennedy from exercising his initiatives.

No man is superior, unless it was by merit, and no man is inferior, unless by his demerit. ~ William Wingate Sewall

Johnson had two clear goals: revitalizing the economy with tax cuts and ending segregation. His definite purpose and elaborate plan of action led him to success.

The visionary leader leads the nation to groundbreaking discoveries or unfathomable economic growth years ahead of any other country.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Exemplary leaders make it their legacy to be a force for positive change. They have persisted in this resolve even in the most unsavory conditions: war, famine, or both. They employ various leadership styles in unique and productive modes of application.

Transformational leadership is an effective economic, cultural, and social change tool. Most nations are stuck because their leaders need the right mindset to transform their minds and lives. While some leaders inspire their citizens to achieve a common goal, others appeal to the citizens’ interests through transactional leadership.

The transactional leadership style falls short in a crisis and calls upon crisis management leadership to handle chaotic situations. When the problem is solved, turnaround leadership returns the country to its former glory. Last but not least, a leader must not be without a vision — a clear goal for his administration. A visionary leader makes relevant decisions that ensure a better future. Every situation calls for adequate and effective leadership, and there are specific leadership styles for every situation. So, it is essential to apply them appropriately. However, leaders need to emulate and practice all forms of leadership.

Try this

If you want to become a good leader, it is essential to take the initiative; you must possess critical thinking and practical listening skills. But don't just assume you’re a good leader. Ask the people you lead to rate your leadership on a scale of 1-10, which areas they think are your strengths and which need work. Take their suggestions, especially the recurring ones, seriously and commit to improving.

Read it till the end?
